common_name: Samurai Orchid (Fuukiran, Wind Orchid, Japanese Fan Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on bark, in a small slotted pot with moss, or in the traditional Japanese method of loose sphagnum moss in a ceramic pot; Neofinetia falcata is one of the most cold-hardy of all epiphytic orchids and will survive winter temperatures to 20°F when properly established, making it suited for zone 7-12 outdoor culture in all but the coldest climates. In zone 9, it can be grown outdoors year-round on shaded porches or attached to tree bark. The flowers are small (0.75 inch), pure white, and intensely fragrant with a spicy-sweet vanilla-jasmine scent that is strongest at night. Treasured in Japan for centuries as a formal horticultural art. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial morning sun, mounted on bark, in loose sphagnum moss, or in the traditional Japanese manner in a small ceramic pot with loose moss — Samurai Orchid is remarkably cold-tolerant for a tropical epiphyte and adapts to year-round outdoor culture in zone 8-12 with appropriate moisture and light. Neofinetia falcata is a miniature, monopodial, epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to Japan (Ryukyu Islands, Kyushu, Shikoku), Korea, and China — one of the most historically significant and culturally revered orchids in East Asian horticulture. The plant has been cultivated in Japan as a formal horticultural art for over 300 years, with samurai-class collectors developing hundreds of named cultivars (especially variegated-leaved forms called Fuukiran) and specialized cultivation practices that persist to the present day. The plant is small and compact — the fan-shaped rosette of stiff, curved, two-ranked, gray-green leaves rarely exceeds 6 inches in height or spread. In summer, short racemes of 3–7 small (0.75-inch) pure white, long-spurred flowers emerge from the leaf axils. The flowers are the plant's outstanding quality: the long, curving nectary spur (up to 2 inches) and pure white coloration are beautiful, but the fragrance is extraordinary — an intensely sweet, spicy, vanilla-and-jasmine scent produced in the evening hours and capable of perfuming a room or patio. The cold-hardiness (surviving frost to 20°F in established form), small size, fragrance, and cultural history make Neofinetia falcata one of the most appealing miniature orchids for zone 8-12 outdoor culture. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Neofinetia falcata (Fuukiran): cultivation guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Neofinetia falcata: Japanese fan orchid culture
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Neofinetia falcata species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Neofinetia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Fragrant Aerides (Fox Tail Orchid, Cat's Tail Orchid, Foxtail Orchid)

planting.note: Grow mounted on rough bark or in a slatted basket with coarse bark in bright indirect to partial direct light; Aerides odorata is a large, terete-leaved vandaceous orchid from tropical Asia that produces pendant racemes of densely packed, waxy, white-and-rose-purple flowers with a strong, sweet fragrance. One of the most fragrant orchids in cultivation. In zone 9-12, it can be grown outdoors on shaded porches in summer with protection from frost in winter. The pendant flower spikes with their densely packed, jewel-like flowers are extremely ornamental. Water regularly in active growth; reduce in winter. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning light mounted on bark or in a slatted basket with very open drainage — Fragrant Aerides grows most vigorously in warm, humid, bright conditions similar to its native lowland tropical forest habitat in Southeast Asia. Aerides odorata is a large, upright, monopodial epiphytic orchid in the Orchidaceae family native to India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, the Philippines, and Indonesia — a member of the tribe Vandeae and closely related to Vanda, sharing the same terete (round, cylindrical) leaf form and the same cultural requirements. The plant produces a main stem clothed in distichous (two-ranked), cylindrical, fleshy, dark-green leaves 8–10 inches long that give the plant an unusual, tropical appearance distinct from the broad, flat-leaved vandaceous types. In late spring and summer, long, pendant racemes 8–14 inches long emerge from the leaf axils, densely packed with 20–40 small (1-inch), waxy, long-lasting flowers in white with rose-purple to magenta tips and spotting, and a prominent, spurred, bright rose-purple lip — the densely packed flowers giving the raceme a lush, jewel-studded quality. The fragrance is the outstanding quality: intensely sweet, penetrating, and floral, among the strongest and most pleasant scents of any orchid in cultivation. The species name odorata directly references this fragrance. Aerides odorata has contributed its fragrance and flower density to many hybrid vandaceous orchids. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Aerides odorata cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Aerides odorata species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Aerides: terete vandaceous orchids for warm climates
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Aerides (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Papillilamina Anthurium (Black Velvet Anthurium, Dark Velvet Anthurium, Panama Velvet Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix with high humidity; Anthurium papillilaminum is an exceptionally velvety anthurium from Panama producing large, oval, heart-shaped leaves in very deep, almost black-green with a pronounced velvety texture from dense papillae covering the upper leaf surface. The velvet quality is among the most pronounced of all cultivated anthuriums and the dark color intensity is exceptional. Requires warm (above 60°F), humid (60%+) conditions and deep shade. A prestigious collector's aroid. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in deep shade in moist, well-drained, chunky aroid mix with high humidity and consistently warm temperatures — Papillilamina Anthurium is one of the most intensely velvety and deeply colored of all the ornamental velvet anthuriums, producing leaves of extraordinary depth of color and texture in the proper conditions. Anthurium papillilaminum is a climbing, hemiepiphytic anthurium in the Araceae family native to Panama, belonging to the section Cardiolonchium — the velvet-leaved anthurium group that includes A. crystallinum, A. clarinervium, A. warocqueanum, and A. regale. The plant produces large, broadly cordate (heart-shaped) leaves 12–20 inches long with a surface that is quite remarkable for the intensity of its velvet quality: the entire upper leaf surface is densely covered in papillae (small, rounded surface protrusions) that scatter light diffusely, creating a matte, velvet-fabric appearance that rivals the famous velvety texture of A. warocqueanum. The color of the leaf is a very deep, saturated, near-black forest-green — darker and more intense than most other velvet anthuriums, giving the leaf an almost black appearance in low light. The leaf undersides are pale to medium green. Primary veins are silver-white and moderately prominent, though less dramatically contrasting than in species like A. regale or A. magnificum. The combination of near-black color and pronounced velvet texture makes A. papillilaminum one of the most dramatically dark-foliaged ornamental aroids available. A prestigious collector's plant for serious aroid enthusiasts. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: International Aroid Society — Anthurium papillilaminum cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium papillilaminum species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Velvet anthuriums for tropical collectors
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Perforated Rhaphidophora (Window Rhaphidophora, Foraminous Rhaphidophora, Hole-Leaf Rhaphidophora)

planting.note: Grow on a moss pole, rough bark board, or tree trunk in partial to deep shade with high humidity; Rhaphidophora foraminifera produces leaves with natural circular to elongated holes (fenestrations) perforating the blade — similar in concept to the holes in Monstera deliciosa but produced through a different morphological mechanism. The holes develop as the leaf matures, often beginning as slits at the leaf margin that become closed holes as the leaf expands. Very fast-growing once established. Requires warm, humid conditions and a rough climbing surface. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow on a rough bark surface or moss pole in partial to deep shade in moist, humid conditions — Perforated Rhaphidophora climbs rapidly when given a rough surface and produces its distinctively perforated, window-like leaves most dramatically when allowed to ascend and provided consistently warm, humid conditions. Rhaphidophora foraminifera is a climbing, hemiepiphytic aroid in the Araceae family native to Southeast Asia (Borneo, Sabah, Malaysia), a member of the genus Rhaphidophora in the tribe Monstereae — the same tribe as Monstera, Epipremnum, and Scindapsus. The plant is grown for the unusual quality of its mature adult leaves: broadly oval to oblong, dark glossy green, with a scattered array of circular to irregular holes (fenestrations or foramina — from which the species name foraminifera, 'bearing holes,' derives) perforating the leaf blade. The holes begin forming in medium-sized, maturing leaves and range from small (0.5 inch) to larger irregular perforations scattered across the leaf surface. The fenestration pattern in R. foraminifera differs from Monstera deliciosa both in hole size and distribution, and in the mechanism of formation, but the visual effect — a glossy, dark-green leaf with natural window-like holes — gives the plant an appearance that immediately suggests an unusual tropical relative of the Swiss Cheese Plant. The plant climbs rapidly with adhesive aerial roots and can cover large surfaces of rough bark or moss poles with attractive, window-paned foliage. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: International Aroid Society — Rhaphidophora foraminifera cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Rhaphidophora foraminifera species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing aroids for Florida tropical gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Rhaphidophora (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Giant Peace Lily (Large Spoon Flower, Cochlear Spathe Peace Lily, Mexican Peace Lily)

planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Spathiphyllum cochlearispathum is a much larger species than the common Peace Lily (S. wallisii / S. floribundum), reaching 4–5 feet in height with large, bold, glossy leaves up to 24 inches long and substantial white spathes. It makes a dramatic statement as a large foliage and flowering specimen for shaded tropical gardens in zone 10-12 or as a large interior specimen. Water consistently, allowing the top soil to partially dry between waterings. CAUTION: toxic to pets — calcium oxalate in all Spathiphyllum parts.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil — Giant Peace Lily thrives in the warm, consistently moist, deeply shaded conditions of a sheltered tropical garden or large interior space in zone 10-12, where its bold, large-leaved clumps and substantial white spathes make a far more dramatic statement than the compact common Peace Lily (S. wallisii). Spathiphyllum cochlearispathum is a large, clumping, rhizomatous perennial aroid in the Araceae family native to southern Mexico and Central America in the Orchidaceae family — one of the largest-growing species in the Spathiphyllum genus, which comprises approximately 40 species of tropical shade aroids widely grown as ornamental plants throughout the world under the collective common name 'Peace Lily.' The plant produces substantial clusters of upright, long-petioled, broad, glossy, dark-green, elliptic to lanceolate leaves 18–24 inches long on petioles 18–24 inches tall, creating a bold, architectural, large-leaved mound 4–5 feet tall and wide. Through the warm months, erect flower spikes emerge from the crown carrying the characteristic Spathiphyllum inflorescence: a large (4–6 inch), white, spoon-shaped spathe surrounding an erect, cylindrical, white to cream-green spadix. The flowers are modestly fragrant. Unlike the compact common Peace Lily grown as an interior plant, S. cochlearispathum in appropriate outdoor conditions creates a substantial, luxuriant tropical presence appropriate to large shaded gardens. CAUTION: toxic to pets if ingested.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Spathiphyllum species for Florida shade gardens
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Spathiphyllum cochlearispathum species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Large shade aroids for zone 10-12 Florida landscapes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Spathiphyllum (toxic to cats and dogs)
